nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; inhabits sandy and rocky bottoms, crevices and ledges of seaward reefs at depths typically 3–50 m, most often shallower than 4 m.

decorPreferences: large caves and crevices sized to conceal the entire body
stable live rock structure (secure rockwork to prevent collapses)
tight-fitting, weighted lid with no gaps

diet.notes: Specialized crustacean predator with close-set pebble-like teeth adapted for crushing hard-shelled prey. Has been documented eating xanthid crabs, shrimp, squid, clams, scallops, mollusks, and sea urchins. In captivity, generally accepts meaty frozen and fresh foods (shrimp, squid, clams, fish flesh); live food may be needed initially for newly wild-caught individuals. Feeding with tongs or a feeding stick is advised to avoid accidental bites. A large meal every few days tends to be preferable to frequent small feedings.

goodWith: aggressive fish (not small enough to swallow)
avoidWith: all crustaceans (shrimp, crabs, lobsters — will be eaten)
very small fish (risk of accidental ingestion)

summary: The Zebra Moray (Gymnomuraena zebra) is generally considered one of the more tractable moray species for the home aquarium, known for its distinctive dark-banded pattern and blunt crustacean-crushing teeth. It tends to be peaceful toward fish tankmates but will eat any crustaceans present.
careNotes:
The Zebra Moray is generally considered among the more aquarium-suitable morays due to its peaceful disposition toward fish and its specialized diet of hard-shelled invertebrates — blunt pebble-like teeth mean it poses little tearing risk to fish tankmates. It has been reported to reach up to 150 cm (about 5 ft) in the wild, though most captive specimens tend to stay in the 60–90 cm range. A 125-gallon (473 L) aquarium is commonly cited as the minimum for a single specimen, but full-grown adults in large tanks may benefit from 250–264 gallons (950–1000 L) or more, especially when kept with tankmates.

A tight-fitting, weighted lid is essential — this species has been documented pushing loose covers aside and escaping. Escape incidents are well-documented across the hobby. Stable, well-secured rockwork is important as the eel tends to rearrange substrate and can topple unsecured structures.

Reef safety is nuanced: the Zebra Moray will consume any crustaceans in the tank (shrimp, crabs, hermit crabs, lobsters) and large specimens may accidentally damage corals or gorgonians due to their bulk and movement — it is generally advised to keep it only with non-crustacean invertebrates. Corals are not actively targeted.

Temperature disagreement noted: aquarium hobby sources commonly recommend 72–78°F (22–26°C), while FishBase wild-habitat data shows 24.7–29.1°C. The range used here (22–28°C) represents an average across sources. Minimum tank size also varied across sources (473–1000 L); 473 L represents the commonly cited beginner minimum for a single specimen.

FishBase notes the species is 'possibly a protogynous hermaphrodite' but this remains unconfirmed. It is not considered venomous; bites can be painful due to jaw strength but are not envenoming. Feeding should be done with tongs or a feeding stick rather than by hand.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been reliably documented. FishBase notes the species may be a protogynous hermaphrodite (female-first sex changer), but this remains unconfirmed. Captive reproduction is generally considered not achieved under typical aquarium conditions.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Product page confirmed: care level moderate, temperament peaceful, reef safe with caution, 125 gal minimum, 72-78°F, SG 1.020-1.025, pH 8.1-8.4.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (150 cm max), taxonomy (Muraenidae, Anguilliformes), native range, wild temperature range (24.7–29.1°C, higher than hobby sources), diet (xanthid crabs and crustaceans). Wild temperature data differs from hobby recommendation — averaged in waterParameters.

additional.name: AquaticCommunity
additional.notes: Cross-verified temperature (72-78°F / 22-26°C), salinity (1.022-1.025), pH (8.1-8.4), adult size (150 cm), carnivore diet. Suggested 250 gal with tankmates. Noted escape-artist behavior requiring weighted lid.

additional.name: ReefApp
additional.notes: Cross-verified adult size (150 cm), minimum tank (1000 L for adults), SG ~1.026, reef-safe-with-caution status. Confirmed escape-artist and rockwork-rearranging behaviors.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable captive breeding reported. Possibly protogynous (FishBase), unconfirmed. Not bred under typical aquarium conditions.
